one_line_takeaway: MalModel turns an app's own on-device DL model into a covert, VirusTotal-invisible malware carrier by stuffing an Android payload into weight mantissa bytes and grafting on a DeepPayload-style trigger, needing no retraining of the host model and succeeding on 41% of real GooglePlay DL apps.
key_finding: MalModel achieves high injection capacity with minimal accuracy loss when model redundancy is sufficient: e.g., injecting AprXhelper (2.09MB) into Squeezenet (4.73MB) costs only 0.4% accuracy; large models (VGG16, ResNet50, InceptionV3) show 0% accuracy change even for the 9.63MB mobok payload. Injected apps are completely undetected by VirusTotal (0/62 for all 7 samples, vs 22-38/~63 for originals and 7-22/~62 after conventional obfuscation), and model-to-grayscale steganalysis scores are indistinguishable from clean (0.5 vs 0.5). In real GooglePlay apps, 24/58 (41%) were successfully attacked. Overhead is asymmetric: per-call INFERENCE latency is small (<=39ms), but one-time INITIALIZATION latency can reach several seconds (e.g., ResNet50+mobok: 788ms -> 7675ms), which the authors argue is amortizable via multithreading.
core_contribution: MalModel is an offensive method for hiding executable Android malware (a .dex payload) inside the FP32 weight parameters of on-device mobile deep learning models, plus a structural backdoor that triggers the payload conditionally at runtime. The injection replaces the last 1-3 bytes (mantissa) of selected weights, guided by a strategy balancing four factors: layer type (dense preferred over convolutional), layer number (fewer layers preferred), neuron/layer coverage (lower coverage preferred), and number of bytes replaced (1-2 preferred over 3). Crucially, the TARGET model is not retrained -- injection is 'black-box' in the sense that it needs no training data and treats the model as opaque, which is also why the payload evades scanners that see models as black boxes. The backdoor is built by EXTENDING DeepPayload [29] (a cited prior work, not this paper's invention): the authors reuse DeepPayload's resize operator and trigger detector but contribute a modified merge module that preserves the original model output (multiplying it by a reshaped constant 1) so the host app behaves normally until the trigger fires. Note the trigger detector itself IS trained (a small CNN) on public data; only the host model is left untouched.
threat_model: The attacker possesses a benign mobile application that ships an on-device DL model (e.g., for image classification, face recognition, traffic-sign detection). The attacker decompiles the app, extracts the model file, embeds the malicious dex payload into the model weights via MalModel, injects a backdoor branch, adds a small dynamic-loading stub to the DL framework (using Android InMemoryDexClassLoader + Java reflection), and repacks the APK. At runtime the modified framework checks each input for the trigger (e.g., motion blur, reflection, or eye-glasses); when detected it extracts the payload from the weights, verifies its md5, loads it in memory, and invokes its entry point. Goal: run malicious logic (SMS exfiltration, file upload/download, screenshots, shell commands) while evading static/dynamic anti-malware detection, since the code logic is unchanged and the payload lives in opaque model parameters.
limitations: Stated by the authors (Sec 6.2): (1) the injected backdoor branch is a conspicuous structural addition distinguishable from the original model graph -- a defender scanning model structure can spot it; (2) the attack cannot survive further parameter modification -- fine-tuning or re-quantizing the model destroys the payload; (3) it is unsuitable for highly compressed/quantized models (e.g., 8-bit integer weights) because low parameter redundancy limits capacity. Additional observed limits: injecting large payloads into small models can be catastrophic (mobok 9.63MB drops MobileNetV2 to 0.1% and MobileNet to 0.2%; small models like Squeezenet cannot hold the largest samples at all); multi-layer injection with 3-byte replacement collapses accuracy (MobileNetV2 -> ~0%); the implementation mainly targets image-classification models, and of the 34 failed real-world attacks the causes were multiple I/O nodes (18), model decoding/encryption errors (7), unsupported operators (6), and non-image model types (3).

datasets_used: ImageNet validation set (2,000 images used to measure post-injection model accuracy)
imagenette (13,394 samples; used to build reflection and motion-blur trigger training data)
CelebA (13,193 face images with glasses + 13,193 without; used to train the eye-glasses specific trigger detector)
AndroidMalware_2020 (source of the 7 injected malware samples: anubis 514KB, AprCerberus 1.12MB, AprXhelper 2.09MB, PremiumRateSMS 3.16MB, BankerSpanish 5.52MB, EventBot 6.03MB, mobok 9.63MB)
Malware DB (source of 6 samples used in the baseline comparison: EquationDrug 372KB, NSIS 1.7MB, Mamba 2.3MB, WannaCry 3.4MB, VikingHorde 7.1MB, Artemis 12.8MB)
43,507 GooglePlay apps crawled (Jan 2023), filtered to 58 on-device TensorFlow DL apps for real-world feasibility
quantitative_results: Injecting AprXhelper (2.09MB) into Squeezenet (4.73MB): only 0.4% accuracy drop (55.2% -> 54.85%)
Large models VGG16/ResNet50/InceptionV3: 0% accuracy change across all 7 malware including 9.63MB mobok
Catastrophic drops on small models with largest payload: mobok -> MobileNetV2 0.1%, MobileNet 0.2%; Squeezenet cannot inject BankerSpanish/EventBot/mobok
Trigger detector performance -- reflection: acc 0.9719 / prec 0.9494 / recall 0.9969; motion blur: acc 0.9281 / prec 0.9597 / recall 0.8938; eye glasses: acc 0.9734 / prec 0.9690 / recall 0.9781
VirusTotal detections: MalModel 0/62 for all 7 samples; originals 22/62-38/62; AVPASS obfuscation 7/62-22/63
Steganalysis (StegoExpose fusion score): model-to-grayscale MalModel constant 0.5 for clean and all malware (indistinguishable); openstego images rise 0.035 (clean) -> up to 0.30 (detectable)
Inference latency overhead <=39ms; one-time initialization latency up to ~6.9s (ResNet50+mobok init 788ms -> 7675ms)
Real-world feasibility: 24/58 (41%) GooglePlay DL apps successfully attacked

Injection strategy validation: dense layers generally beat convolutional (e.g., InceptionV3 75% vs 73.45%); 2-byte replacement preserves accuracy far better than 3-byte (MobileNetV2 5-conv: 65.35% at 2 bytes vs 0.65% at 3 bytes)
Capacity formula example: a model with a 192-param conv layer + 1600-param dense layer can hold up to 921,600 bytes
baselines_compared: EvilModel [50] (fast substitution + retraining) -- injection-capacity/accuracy baseline in Fig. 5
StegoNet [30] (4 injection methods) -- injection-capacity/accuracy baseline in Fig. 5
DeepPayload [29] -- NOT a competitor but the prior backdoor technique this paper extends (reused resize operator + trigger detector; modified merge module)
